# AAB Data Cleaning Pipeline
**Project:** Sleep Disruption as an Endophenotype of Autism (FamilySleeps)  
**Author:** Mailton Vasconcelos  
**Institution:** Maynooth University  
**Data source:** Australian Autism Biobank (AAB) — Access Request 2024-047  
**Data dictionary version:** 2025-05-14  
**Script:** `Get-vars-new_20260328v02.R`  
**Last updated:** 2026-03-28

---

### Overview

This pipeline cleans and prepares the AAB dataset for the FamilySleeps endophenotype analysis. It starts from the raw REDCap export and produces a cleaned dataset (`AAB_data`) with derived variables ready for analysis. A safe copy of the raw data (`AAB_safe_copy`) is preserved throughout.

---

## Block 1: Demographic variables

### Participant type

`participant_type` recoded to a labelled factor (`participant_type_f`):
1 = Proband, 2 = ASD-Q, 3 = Sibling, 4 = Control, 5 = Mother, 6 = Father.

Only rows with no `id` at all are dropped (genuine empty REDCap rows). Rows
with a valid `id` but missing `participant_type` are retained and inspected
before any decision is made.

### Age recovery (children)

Age was computed from date pairs in the following priority order:

1. `proforma_dob` + `proforma_doa`
2. `ados_dob` + `ados_doe`
3. `ados_dob` + `vabs_testdate`
4. `vabs_dob` + `acl_childsample_date`
5. `vabs_dob` + `vabs_testdate`
6. `acl_child_dob` + `acl_proforma_date`
7. `acl_child_dob` + `acl_cog_date`
8. `acl_child_dob` + `acl_childsample_date`

**Result:** 1573/1579 children recovered (6 still missing — no date information available in any field).

All intermediate parsed date columns and the `age_at_assessment_clean` /
`age_parent` intermediates are dropped at the end of the age block.

### Age recovery (parents)

Parent age derived from `fhq_b9` (mother DOB) and `fhq_b24_dob` (father DOB),
paired with the proforma assessment date from the child's row. A unified `age`
variable covers all six participant types.

### Sex recovery (children)

Sex derived in priority order: `proforma_sex` → `fhq_b2` → `ados_gender` (recoded).

> **Note:** `ados_gender` uses reversed coding relative to `proforma_sex`
> (ADOS 1 = Female, ADOS 2 = Male). Recoded before use.

Sex assigned deterministically for enrolled parents: Mother = Female, Father = Male.

**Result:** 10 still missing across children (5 probands, 4 siblings, 1 control).

### Site

No `site` variable in the raw export. Derived from participant ID prefix:

| Prefix | Site |
|--------|------|
| 11 | WA (Telethon Kids Institute) |
| 22 | VIC (La Trobe University) |
| 33 | NSW (University of New South Wales) |
| 44 | QLD (Lady Cilento Children's Hospital) |

---

## Block 2: Diagnosis confirmation and grouping

`fhq_h1` records the diagnosis type for probands and ASD-Q participants only
(NA by design for siblings and controls).

**Coding:** 1=Autism, 2=PDD-NOS, 3=Asperger's Syndrome, 4=Other, 5=999 (data entry error).

Code 5 recoded to NA. Code 4 (Other) reviewed case-by-case via `fhq_h1_detail`.
10 cases with no valid ASD diagnosis identified and recoded to NA.

**ASD-Q participants (participant_type = 2, n=15):** Only 1 of 15 had a
confirmed diagnosis. All 15 excluded from `diagnosis_group` and retained
in dataset with NA. Excluded from main analysis; may be considered in
sensitivity analyses.

---

## Block 3: CSHQ — Children's Sleep Habits Questionnaire

CSHQ scores computed from `fhq_h16_a` through `fhq_h16_ah` (34 items).
Items coded 999 recoded to NA. Six positively-worded items reverse-scored
(new variables with `_r` suffix created, originals preserved).

**Reverse-scored items:** `fhq_h16_a`, `fhq_h16_b`, `fhq_h16_g`, `fhq_h16_i`,
`fhq_h16_j`, `fhq_h16_aa` (recoded as 4 − x, so 1→3, 2→2, 3→1).

**Subscales (8):** Bedtime Resistance (6 items), Sleep Onset Delay (1 item),
Sleep Duration (3 items), Sleep Anxiety (4 items), Night Wakings (3 items),
Parasomnias (7 items), Sleep-Disordered Breathing (3 items),
Daytime Sleepiness (8 items).

**Total score:** 33 unique items summed directly (not sum of subscales).
`fhq_h16_l` (Afraid of sleeping alone) excluded from total — duplicate of
`fhq_h16_f` in Bedtime Resistance per Owens et al. (2000).
`fhq_h16_d` (Needs parent in room) appears in both Bedtime Resistance and
Sleep Anxiety subscales but counted once in total score.

### Missingness

Missingness is consistent across sites (27–35% for probands) and is not
explained by FHQ completion status alone — 226 participants had FHQ completed
but CSHQ still missing, suggesting the sleep section was sometimes skipped.

Of the 241 partially completed questionnaires:
- 213 missing ≤ 3 items → prorated
- 28 missing > 3 items → retained as NA
- 284 missing all 33 items → genuinely no data

Missing items are distributed randomly across all 33 items with no systematic
pattern. `fhq_h16_q` (bed wetting) and `fhq_h16_m` (trouble sleeping away
from home) are the most commonly skipped single items, likely left blank when
not applicable.

### Prorating

`cshq_total_prorated` computed for cases missing ≤ 3 items:
```r
prorated_score = observed_sum * (33 / n_items_present)
```

Use `cshq_total_prorated` as the primary outcome variable in all analyses.
`cshq_total` (unprorated) retained for sensitivity analysis.

> **Note:** The `sleep_` prefixed variables were generated for the paper
> "Clinical and behavioral attributes leading to sleep disorders in children
> on the autism spectrum" led by Dr Anne Masi. Any publications using these
> variables must acknowledge the original authors.
> Contact: Professor Valsamma Eapen (v.eapen@unsw.edu.au).

**Reference:** Owens, J. A., Spirito, A., & McGuinn, M. (2000). The Children's
Sleep Habits Questionnaire (CSHQ): Psychometric properties of a survey
instrument for school-aged children. *Sleep, 23*(8), 1–9.
https://doi.org/10.1093/sleep/23.8.1d

---

## Block 4: Family ID derivation

No family identifier or pedigree variable was present in the raw export.
Family clusters were derived from `acl_mother_id` and `acl_father_id`
recorded against each child, using the mother ID as the primary family
anchor (higher completeness), with father ID as fallback in single-father
families. Fathers in two-parent families inherited the mother anchor.
Comparison children received unique singleton family IDs to allow
participation in mixed-effects models without spurious clustering.

Unenrolled parents are apparently a design feature of the AAB — approximately 52%
of recorded mother IDs and 63% of recorded father IDs have no
corresponding participant row in the dataset. Their IDs serve as family
anchors regardless of enrolment status and do not affect linkage.

**Unlinked participants.** 107 participants (105 probands, 2 siblings)
could not be assigned a family ID because no parent ID was recorded at
all (`acl_mother_id` and `acl_father_id` both absent). This is distinct
from unenrolled parents — here the linkage field itself is empty. 104 of
105 unlinked probands are from the WA site, suggesting a data entry
omission at that collection site.

> **Action required:** Contact AAB data team to (1) clarify whether
> parent IDs exist in REDCap for the 107 WA participants with no linkage
> fields recorded, (2) request `participant_twin` and
> `participant_multiplex` variables, (3) request a pre-existing
> family/pedigree ID for validation, and (4) request cognitive assessment
> data (WISC, Mullen, WASI scores).

---

## Block 5: Melatonin use variables

Melatonin use was derived by scanning all free-text medication name fields
across the "Prescribed Medications" (`fhq_h13_[a–f]`) and "Natural Therapies"
(`fhq_h14_[a–f]`) sections of the FHQ, plus the catch-all notes field
(`fhq_h13_other`). A regex helper flagged melatonin mentions across common
spellings, typos, and brand names (e.g., Circadin, Melatone). Eight
participant-level variables were derived: a binary ever-use flag
(`melatonin_ever`), current or PRN use (`melatonin_current`), age at first
use (`melatonin_age_first`), raw duration string (`melatonin_duration_raw`),
perceived improvement (`melatonin_improved`), medication source section
(`melatonin_source`), and a flag for participants whose melatonin detection
came from a concatenated free-text entry (`melatonin_entry_concat`).
Participants flagged as `melatonin_entry_concat` have reliable `melatonin_ever`
status but unreliable ancillary fields and should be excluded from analyses
using age, duration, or current-use variables. Age at first use required
cleaning to remove calendar years (e.g., "2017") and implausible values
(> 30 years) erroneously entered in the free-text field.

---

## Block 6: Pubertal staging (Tanner genital score)

Pubertal status was assessed via clinician-administered Tanner staging
(`tanner` form). Following Emmanuel & Bokor (2022) and Yap et al. (2023),
who used the same variable in the AAB, **genital/breast development was
used as the sole domain** (`tanner_boy_genital` for males,
`tanner_girl_genital` for females). A single unified variable (`tanner_genital`)
was derived across all child participants (types 1–4). An ordered factor
(`tanner_genital_f`, Stages 1–5) was also created for use in ordinal models.

**Sex concordance check.** A `tanner_sex_mismatch` flag is computed first by
comparing `tanner_sex` against the derived `sex` variable. A new intermediate
variable `tanner_sex_resolved` is then created — using the concordant
`tanner_sex` value where available, falling back to the derived `sex` where
`tanner_sex` is absent, and setting NA for any mismatched case. `tanner_genital`
is mapped from `tanner_sex_resolved`, not directly from `tanner_sex`.

One participant (ID 1131608, potentially a female sibling) had `tanner_sex = 1`
(Boys) recorded and only boy-scale items completed. Because genital and breast
development cannot be mapped across sexes, `tanner_genital` was set to NA for
this participant. Chromosomal sex verification via PLINK is planned.

Both `tanner_sex_mismatch` and `tanner_sex_resolved` are dropped after
`tanner_genital` is computed — they do not appear in the final dataset.

**References:**  
Emmanuel M, Bokor BR. Tanner Stages. [Updated 2022 Dec 11]. In: StatPearls
[Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2026 Jan-.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470280/?report=classic  
Yap, C.X., Henders, A.K., Alvares, G.A. et al. Interactions between the
lipidome and genetic and environmental factors in autism. Nat Med 29, 936–949
(2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-023-02271-1

---

## CONSIDER: Social Responsiveness Score for Siblings and Comparison children

**Genetic Correlation Analysis**
The SRS total T score provides a continuous, norm-referenced measure of autistic
trait severity in siblings and comparison children, enabling variance-component
genetic correlation estimation between sleep traits and autism liability —
something the binary proband diagnosis alone cannot support.

**Intermediate Expression in Relatives**
If siblings score intermediate on SRS total T score between probands and
comparison children — alongside intermediate CSHQ scores — that convergent
pattern across two independent phenotypic dimensions substantially strengthens
the endophenotype case beyond what sleep data alone could show.

---

## Block 7: Ethnicity

Ethnicity was reported by parents at enrolment via the FHQ (maternal: `fhq_b8`;
paternal: `fhq_b23`). Maternal ethnicity was assigned as the child's proxy
(Vedelli et al., 2024), with paternal ethnicity as fallback when maternal was
absent (n = 18). As the FHQ is completed once per family, ethnicity was
propagated to children with no value in their own row by inheriting from another
child in the same family (n = 212 recovered). Lastly, 129 families (11.4%) had
discordant parental ethnicity; these are flagged via `ethnicity_discordant` for
use in sensitivity analyses.

A 4-level factor for use as covariate in regression models was derived:
Caucasian (reference), Asian, Indigenous/Pacific, Other. Aboriginal and
Maori/Pacific Islander were combined into `"Indigenous/Pacific"` due to small
cell sizes (n ≈ 11 and n ≈ 14 respectively). This grouping is explicitly
acknowledged as a limitation — per NHMRC guidelines, Aboriginal and Torres
Strait Islander peoples should be identified separately where sample size
permits.

A binary sensitivity variable (`ethnicity_binary`: Caucasian vs Non-Caucasian)
is also retained for robustness checks in subgroup analyses.

**Note:** Genomic ancestry PCs will replace self-reported ethnicity in all
genetic analyses.

**References:**
National Health and Medical Research Council (Australia). (2018). Ethical
conduct in research with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples and
communities: Guidelines for researchers and stakeholders. NHMRC.  
Vedelli, L., Azizi, S., & Anand, K. J. S. (2024). Race and ethnicity data in
paediatric research. JAMA Pediatrics, 178(1), 6–8.
https://doi.org/10.1001/jamapediatrics.2023.4745

---

## Block 8: SES composite variable

SES was operationalised at the family level from two FHQ components: family
income (`fhq_b36`) and maximum parental education (`edu_max`, derived as
`pmax(fhq_b10, fhq_b25, na.rm = TRUE)`). Both were derived from the proband
row and propagated to all family members via `family_id`.

The family-level lookup table (`family_ses_raw`) is built, composites computed,
and then immediately joined back to `AAB_data` before `rm(family_ses_raw)` is
called. The intermediate columns `edu_mother` and `edu_father` are dropped from
`AAB_data` after the join.

The primary composite (`ses_composite_complete`) is the equal-weight mean of
`income_z` and `edu_max_z`, complete cases only. A partial composite
(`ses_composite_partial`) allowing one missing component is retained for
sensitivity analyses, with `ses_n_components` flagging how many components
contributed.

Occupation (`fhq_b12`, `fhq_b27`) can be considered pending ANZSCO coding.

A postcode request has been submitted to the AAB data team to enable SEIFA IRSAD
linkage, which will replace `ses_composite_complete` as the primary SES covariate
once received. The ceiling limitation of the current composite should be noted
when interpreting SES effects.

**Reference:**
Avvisati, F. The measure of socio-economic status in PISA: a review and some
suggested improvements. Large-scale Assess Educ 8, 8 (2020).
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40536-020-00086-x

---

## Dependencies

```r
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(stringr)
library(visdat)
library(purrr)
```

---

## Notes for collaborators

- Always start from the raw CSV — do not overwrite it.
- `AAB_safe_copy` preserves the raw data within the session.
- All intermediate variables are removed at the end of each cleaning block.
- The retired script `Get-vars-new_20260316v01.R` is in `archive/` for reference only — do not use it.

## A note on language

This project uses identity-first language (e.g. "autistic individuals") and
avoids pathologising framing in line with preferences expressed by the autistic
community and our Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) contributors.

The comparison group is referred to as **"Comparison"** rather than
"typically developing", "neurotypical", or "controls" — terms that imply
a normative standard against which autistic people are measured.

Where clinical or diagnostic terminology is required (e.g. DSM-5 criteria,
ADOS-2 scores), it is used precisely and with context.

This language policy applies to all outputs from the FamilySleeps project
including reports, presentations and publications.
